topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s pronouncement hung in the stale air of the drawing-room like a dead weight. "The house," he’d stated, his voice dry as autumn leaves, "is yours, jointly." I stared at the ornate, dusty chandelier above, the crystal catching the weak afternoon light in a way that felt mocking. My brother, Mark, was already halfway to the door, his easy grin already plastered on his face, a performance I resented watching. I didn't move. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails dug crescents into my palms. Every inherited item—the chipped porcelain, the faded velvet—felt like a physical tether to a past I desperately wanted to sever. I walked to the window, the glass cool against my forehead, and watched the manicured lawn, feeling a profound emptiness settle deep in my chest, a cold, hard knot that refused to loosen.

[story 2]

I traced the peeling floral wallpaper with a fingertip, the texture rough beneath my skin. The deed was signed, the transfer complete, and suddenly the entire estate felt like a cage built of expectations I never wanted. Mark was already downstairs, laughing at some trivial anecdote, his voice bright and careless. I remained standing in the hallway, arms crossed so tightly my shoulders ached. I took a slow, shallow breath, the scent of aged wood and lavender doing nothing to soothe the tightness coiling in my stomach. Why did I have to share this? It was all just more obligation, another thing to manage, another burden placed squarely on my shoulders. I turned away from the parlor, the silence of the grand house amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears, a rhythm entirely of my own displeasure.

[story 3]

The key felt heavy, cold, and utterly meaningless in my hand. This sprawling, decaying mansion—Grandmother’s legacy—was now mine, and the sheer weight of ownership pressed down on me like a physical mantle. I surveyed the cavernous entrance hall, noting the undisturbed layers of dust on the mahogany banister. I didn't bother to unpack; there was nothing here worth touching, nothing that held any warmth. When Mark finally appeared, radiating an almost irritating cheerfulness, I offered a clipped nod. "Right," I managed, my tone flat, devoid of any welcoming inflection. I moved toward the stairs, each step a deliberate effort against the inertia of the situation, my gaze fixed on the shadows pooling in the corners, a silent declaration that I would inhabit this space only as a necessary inconvenience.
